primitives.name: Dimensional Analysis
primitives.formalStatement: Every physical equation must be dimensionally consistent: both sides must have the same dimensions in terms of fundamental quantities (length L, mass M, time T, charge Q, temperature Theta). A quantity Q has dimensions [Q] = L^a M^b T^c Q^d Theta^e.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Express each quantity in terms of fundamental dimensions. 2. Verify both sides of an equation have identical dimensional exponents. 3. Use to derive relationships: if f depends on variables with known dimensions, dimensional analysis constrains the functional form.

dependencies.description: Dimensional analysis operates on physical quantities with units

compositionRules.yields: Systematic derivation of dimensionless groups governing a physical system
conditions: Physical system with known relevant variables
compositionRules.example: Pendulum period depends on L, g: [T] = [L]^a [LT^{-2}]^b gives a=1/2, b=-1/2, so T ~ sqrt(L/g)

applicabilityPatterns: Check if a physics equation is dimensionally consistent
Derive the functional form of a physical relationship from dimensional constraints
Determine which variables a physical quantity can depend on
buildLabs: Build Lab 15.1: Dimensional Analysis in Practice

primitives.name: Buckingham Pi Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: If a physical system involves n variables and k independent fundamental dimensions, then the system can be described by n - k independent dimensionless groups (Pi groups). Any dimensionless combination is a product of powers of these Pi groups.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. List n variables and k fundamental dimensions. 2. Form the dimensional matrix. 3. Find rank = k'. 4. Number of Pi groups = n - k'. 5. Choose k' repeating variables; form each Pi group by combining remaining variables with repeating ones to cancel dimensions.

dependencies.description: Pi theorem formalizes dimensional analysis into a systematic method

compositionRules.yields: Complete dimensional reduction of any physical problem to dimensionless form
conditions: Complete list of relevant variables known
compositionRules.example: Drag on a sphere: variables F, rho, v, D, mu (5 vars, 3 dims) -> 2 Pi groups: C_D and Re

applicabilityPatterns: Reduce a physical problem to its essential dimensionless parameters
Determine the number of independent dimensionless groups in a system
Systematically construct dimensionless ratios from physical variables

primitives.name: Speed of Light
primitives.formalStatement: The speed of light in vacuum is a universal constant: c = 299,792,458 m/s exactly. It is the maximum speed for information transfer and the conversion factor between space and time: E = mc^2.
primitives.computationalForm: c = 2.998 x 10^8 m/s. Use in energy-mass: E = mc^2. In relativity: gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - v^2/c^2). In natural units: set c = 1, then length and time have same units.

compositionRules.yields: Photon energy: E = hc/lambda, linking quantum and electromagnetic scales

applicabilityPatterns: Convert between mass and energy
Compute relativistic effects at high velocities
Set the scale for electromagnetic phenomena

primitives.name: Planck's Constant
primitives.formalStatement: Planck's constant h = 6.62607015 x 10^{-34} J*s (exact, SI definition). The reduced Planck constant hbar = h/(2*pi). It is the quantum of action, setting the scale where quantum effects become significant.
primitives.computationalForm: h = 6.626 x 10^{-34} J*s. hbar = 1.055 x 10^{-34} J*s. Energy of a photon: E = hf = hbar*omega. De Broglie wavelength: lambda = h/p.

compositionRules.yields: Natural unit system where hbar = c = 1
compositionRules.example: In natural units: energy in eV, length in 1/eV, time in 1/eV

applicabilityPatterns: Determine the scale at which quantum effects matter
Compute photon energy from frequency
Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of a particle

primitives.name: Gravitational Constant
primitives.formalStatement: Newton's gravitational constant G = 6.674 x 10^{-11} N*m^2/kg^2 governs the strength of gravitational interaction: F = G*m_1*m_2/r^2.
primitives.computationalForm: G = 6.674 x 10^{-11} m^3 kg^{-1} s^{-2}. Gravitational force: F = Gm_1m_2/r^2. Gravitational potential energy: U = -Gm_1m_2/r. Escape velocity: v = sqrt(2GM/r).

compositionRules.yields: Schwarzschild radius: r_s = 2GM/c^2, boundary of a black hole

applicabilityPatterns: Compute gravitational forces between masses
Calculate orbital parameters and escape velocities
Determine when general relativistic corrections are needed

primitives.name: Boltzmann Constant
primitives.formalStatement: The Boltzmann constant k_B = 1.380649 x 10^{-23} J/K (exact, SI definition) relates temperature to energy: the average kinetic energy per degree of freedom is (1/2)*k_B*T.
primitives.computationalForm: k_B = 1.381 x 10^{-23} J/K. Thermal energy: E_th = k_B*T. At room temp (300K): k_B*T ≈ 0.026 eV. Entropy: S = k_B * ln(W).

compositionRules.yields: Planck distribution: n(omega) = 1/(exp(hbar*omega/(k_B*T)) - 1), quantum statistics
compositionRules.example: Blackbody peak: hbar*omega_max ≈ 2.82 * k_B * T (Wien's displacement law)

applicabilityPatterns: Convert between temperature and energy scales
Compute thermal fluctuation energies
Determine entropy from microstates

primitives.name: Elementary Charge
primitives.formalStatement: The elementary charge e = 1.602176634 x 10^{-19} C (exact, SI definition) is the magnitude of the charge of the electron and proton. It is the fundamental quantum of electric charge.
primitives.computationalForm: e = 1.602 x 10^{-19} C. Coulomb force: F = ke*q_1*q_2/r^2 where k_e = 1/(4*pi*epsilon_0). Energy unit: 1 eV = e * 1 V = 1.602 x 10^{-19} J.

compositionRules.yields: Fine structure constant: alpha = e^2/(4*pi*epsilon_0*hbar*c) ≈ 1/137, strength of electromagnetic interaction

applicabilityPatterns: Compute electromagnetic forces between charged particles
Convert between energy in joules and electron-volts
Quantize charge in atomic and molecular systems

primitives.name: Fine Structure Constant
primitives.formalStatement: The fine structure constant alpha = e^2/(4*pi*epsilon_0*hbar*c) ≈ 1/137.036 is a dimensionless constant characterizing the strength of the electromagnetic interaction. It appears throughout atomic physics and quantum electrodynamics.
primitives.computationalForm: alpha ≈ 1/137.036 ≈ 0.007297. Appears in: Bohr model energy levels E_n = -alpha^2*m_e*c^2/(2n^2), hydrogen fine structure splitting, QED perturbation theory (corrections in powers of alpha).

dependencies.description: alpha is defined in terms of e
alpha is defined in terms of hbar
alpha is defined in terms of c

compositionRules.yields: Hydrogen energy levels: E_n = -13.6 eV / n^2, with fine structure corrections of order alpha^2
compositionRules.example: Ground state hydrogen: E_1 = -alpha^2*m_e*c^2/2 = -13.6 eV

applicabilityPatterns: Characterize the strength of electromagnetic interactions
Compute perturbative corrections in QED
Determine the fine structure splitting of atomic energy levels

primitives.name: Natural Units (Planck Units)
primitives.formalStatement: Natural units set fundamental constants to 1: hbar = c = k_B = 1 (particle physics) or hbar = c = G = 1 (Planck units). Planck length l_P = sqrt(hbar*G/c^3) ≈ 1.6 x 10^{-35} m, Planck mass m_P = sqrt(hbar*c/G) ≈ 2.2 x 10^{-8} kg, Planck time t_P = l_P/c ≈ 5.4 x 10^{-44} s.
primitives.computationalForm: Set hbar = c = 1: energy, mass, and inverse-length share units (typically GeV). Restore SI: multiply by appropriate powers of hbar and c. Planck scales mark where quantum gravity becomes relevant.

dependencies.description: Natural units exploit dimensional analysis to simplify equations
c = 1 in natural units
hbar = 1 in natural units

compositionRules.yields: Planck scales: the natural units of quantum gravity where l_P, m_P, t_P emerge
conditions: All three constants c, hbar, G combined
compositionRules.example: Planck energy: E_P = m_P*c^2 = sqrt(hbar*c^5/G) ≈ 1.22 x 10^{19} GeV

applicabilityPatterns: Simplify equations in particle physics by setting constants to 1
Identify the fundamental scales where quantum gravity becomes relevant
Convert between natural and SI units

primitives.name: Dimensional Homogeneity
primitives.formalStatement: Every physically meaningful equation must be dimensionally homogeneous: all terms added or equated must have the same dimensions. If [LHS] != [RHS], the equation is physically invalid.
primitives.computationalForm: For each term: determine dimensions in [L^a M^b T^c ...]. Check all terms in a sum or equation have identical dimensional exponents. Use to detect errors in derivations.

dependencies.description: Homogeneity is the core principle of dimensional analysis

compositionRules.yields: Error detection: identify physically impossible equations before computing
compositionRules.example: F = mv is dimensionally wrong: [MLT^{-2}] != [MLT^{-1}]; correct is F = ma

applicabilityPatterns: Verify that a derived equation is physically valid
Detect algebraic errors by checking dimensional consistency
Constrain the form of unknown physical relationships

primitives.name: Physical Quantity
primitives.formalStatement: A physical quantity Q is the product of a numerical value {Q} and a unit [Q]: Q = {Q} x [Q]. The numerical value depends on the choice of unit, but the physical quantity is invariant. Examples: length l = 5 m, energy E = 3.2 eV.
primitives.computationalForm: Always track units alongside numerical values. Convert units by multiplying by appropriate conversion factors (dimensionless ratios equal to 1). SI base units: m, kg, s, A, K, mol, cd.

compositionRules.yields: Complete measurement framework: quantity = number x unit in a coherent system
compositionRules.example: Force of 1 Newton: F = 1 N = 1 kg*m/s^2

applicabilityPatterns: Represent a measurable physical quantity with units
Convert between different unit systems
Ensure calculations preserve physical meaning

primitives.name: SI Unit System
primitives.formalStatement: The International System of Units (SI) defines 7 base units: metre (m), kilogram (kg), second (s), ampere (A), kelvin (K), mole (mol), candela (cd). All other units are derived from these. Since 2019, all base units are defined via fundamental constants.
primitives.computationalForm: Derived units: Newton = kg*m/s^2, Joule = kg*m^2/s^2, Watt = J/s, Volt = J/C, etc. Prefix system: kilo=10^3, mega=10^6, milli=10^{-3}, micro=10^{-6}, nano=10^{-9}.

dependencies.description: SI provides the standard units for physical quantities

compositionRules.yields: Coherent measurement framework where derived units follow naturally from base units
compositionRules.example: 1 Joule = 1 kg * m^2 / s^2, derived from base units

applicabilityPatterns: Express physical measurements in a standardized unit system
Convert between SI and other unit systems
Derive units for complex physical quantities from base units

primitives.name: Atomic Number and Mass
primitives.formalStatement: The atomic number Z is the number of protons in an atom's nucleus, uniquely identifying the element. The mass number A = Z + N where N is the number of neutrons. Atomic mass is measured in unified atomic mass units (u ≈ 1.661 x 10^{-27} kg).
primitives.computationalForm: Z = number of protons = number of electrons (neutral atom). A = Z + N. Isotope notation: ^A_Z X. Atomic mass of an element: weighted average over isotope abundances.

dependencies.description: Nuclear charge is Z*e

compositionRules.yields: Electron shell structure: Z electrons fill orbitals according to quantum numbers
compositionRules.example: Carbon (Z=6): 6 electrons fill 1s^2 2s^2 2p^2

applicabilityPatterns: Identify an element from its number of protons
Determine the number of electrons in a neutral atom
Calculate atomic mass from isotope data

primitives.name: Quantum Numbers
primitives.formalStatement: Each electron in an atom is described by four quantum numbers: principal n (1,2,3,...), angular momentum l (0,...,n-1), magnetic m_l (-l,...,+l), and spin m_s (+1/2 or -1/2). No two electrons can share all four quantum numbers (Pauli exclusion).
primitives.computationalForm: n determines energy level and shell size. l determines orbital shape (s,p,d,f for l=0,1,2,3). m_l determines orbital orientation. m_s determines spin direction. Total electrons in shell n: 2n^2.

dependencies.description: Quantum numbers describe electrons in atoms identified by Z

compositionRules.yields: Shell filling rules: maximum 2 electrons per orbital, 2l+1 orbitals per subshell
compositionRules.example: 3d subshell (n=3, l=2): 5 orbitals (m_l = -2,-1,0,1,2), max 10 electrons

applicabilityPatterns: Describe the state of an electron in an atom
Determine the number of electrons in each shell and subshell
Predict orbital shapes and orientations
buildLabs: Build Lab 16.2: Quantum Numbers and Orbitals

primitives.name: Electron Configuration (Aufbau Principle)
primitives.formalStatement: The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p, 5s, 4d, ... The filling order follows the (n+l) rule: lower n+l fills first; for equal n+l, lower n fills first.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Order subshells by (n+l), breaking ties by n. 2. Fill each subshell with up to 2(2l+1) electrons. 3. Apply Hund's rule within subshells. 4. Note exceptions (Cr, Cu: d-shell stability). Notation: 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 ...

dependencies.description: Filling order depends on quantum numbers n and l
Maximum occupancy per orbital = 2 (one per spin)

compositionRules.yields: Periodic table structure: elements in same column have same valence electron configuration
compositionRules.example: Group 1 (Li, Na, K): all have [core] ns^1 configuration

applicabilityPatterns: Determine the electron configuration of an element
Predict chemical properties from electron arrangement
Understand the structure of the periodic table

primitives.name: Pauli Exclusion Principle
primitives.formalStatement: No two identical fermions (particles with half-integer spin, including electrons) can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously. For electrons in an atom: no two electrons can have the same set of four quantum numbers (n, l, m_l, m_s).
primitives.computationalForm: Maximum electrons per orbital (fixed n, l, m_l): 2 (one spin-up, one spin-down). Maximum per subshell (fixed n, l): 2(2l+1). Maximum per shell (fixed n): 2n^2.

compositionRules.yields: Atomic shell structure: electrons arrange in shells with fixed capacity
compositionRules.example: First shell (n=1): max 2 electrons. Second shell (n=2): max 8. Third (n=3): max 18.

applicabilityPatterns: Determine the maximum number of electrons in a shell or subshell
Explain why atoms have distinct electron shells
Understand the stability of matter (electrons cannot all collapse to lowest state)

primitives.name: Periodic Law and Trends
primitives.formalStatement: When elements are arranged by atomic number, their chemical and physical properties recur periodically. Properties that vary predictably: atomic radius decreases across a period, increases down a group; ionization energy increases across, decreases down; electronegativity increases across, decreases down.
primitives.computationalForm: Periodic trends follow from electron configuration. Across period: increasing Z with same n -> stronger nuclear attraction -> smaller radius. Down group: new shell -> larger radius. Ionization energy correlates inversely with radius.

dependencies.description: Periodic trends are explained by electron configuration patterns
Periodicity follows from arrangement by atomic number

compositionRules.yields: Prediction of chemical behavior: elements with similar valence configurations have similar chemistry
compositionRules.example: Group 17 (halogens F, Cl, Br, I): all ns^2 np^5, all gain one electron readily

applicabilityPatterns: Predict how an element's properties compare to its neighbors
Explain trends in atomic size, ionization energy, or electronegativity
Classify elements by their position and expected behavior

primitives.name: Chemical Bonding
primitives.formalStatement: Chemical bonds form when atoms share or transfer electrons to achieve stable configurations. Ionic bonds: electron transfer between atoms of very different electronegativity. Covalent bonds: electron sharing between atoms of similar electronegativity. Metallic bonds: delocalized electron sharing in metals.
primitives.computationalForm: Bond type prediction: compute electronegativity difference delta_EN. If delta_EN > 1.7: ionic. If delta_EN < 0.4: nonpolar covalent. Between: polar covalent. Bond energy and length are measurable properties.

dependencies.description: Bonding behavior follows from periodic properties
Bond type determined by electronegativity difference
Bonding involves valence electrons

compositionRules.yields: Molecular orbital theory: linear combinations of atomic orbitals form bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals
compositionRules.example: H_2: 1s_A + 1s_B -> sigma (bonding) and sigma* (antibonding); 2 electrons fill sigma

applicabilityPatterns: Determine the type of bond between two atoms
Predict molecular geometry from bonding patterns
Explain the properties of materials from their bonding

primitives.name: Orbital Theory
primitives.formalStatement: An atomic orbital is a mathematical function (wave function) describing the probability amplitude of finding an electron at a given position. The orbital shape is determined by quantum numbers: s-orbitals are spherical, p-orbitals are dumbbell-shaped, d-orbitals have cloverleaf patterns.
primitives.computationalForm: Probability density: P(r,theta,phi) = |psi(r,theta,phi)|^2. Radial probability: P(r) = r^2 |R_{nl}(r)|^2. Angular distribution from spherical harmonics Y_l^m. Orbital energy in hydrogen: E_n = -13.6/n^2 eV.

dependencies.description: Orbital shape and energy determined by quantum numbers

compositionRules.yields: Complete atomic model: wave functions describe electron probability distributions in atoms
compositionRules.example: Hydrogen 1s orbital: psi = (1/sqrt(pi*a_0^3)) exp(-r/a_0)

applicabilityPatterns: Visualize where an electron is likely to be found
Determine the shape and orientation of electron clouds
Understand hybridization and molecular geometry

primitives.name: Electron Shell Model
primitives.formalStatement: Electrons are organized into concentric shells around the nucleus, numbered n = 1, 2, 3, ... (K, L, M, ...). Each shell contains subshells (s, p, d, f) with increasing angular momentum. Maximum electrons per shell: 2n^2.
primitives.computationalForm: Shell n has subshells l = 0, 1, ..., n-1. Each subshell holds 2(2l+1) electrons. Shell 1: 2, Shell 2: 8, Shell 3: 18, Shell 4: 32. Valence shell = outermost occupied shell.

dependencies.description: Shell structure derives from quantum numbers

compositionRules.yields: Fixed shell capacity: 2n^2 electrons per shell, explaining noble gas stability
compositionRules.example: Neon (Z=10): 2+8=10, filling shells 1 and 2 completely -> noble gas stability

applicabilityPatterns: Organize electrons into shells and subshells
Determine which shell is the valence shell
Predict the maximum number of electrons per shell

primitives.name: Electronegativity
primitives.formalStatement: Electronegativity (EN) measures an atom's tendency to attract shared electrons in a bond. On the Pauling scale, fluorine has the highest value (3.98) and francium the lowest (~0.7). EN increases across a period (left to right) and decreases down a group.
primitives.computationalForm: Pauling scale: EN difference predicts bond character. |EN_A - EN_B| > 1.7: ionic. < 0.4: nonpolar covalent. Between: polar covalent. Mulliken EN = (IE + EA)/2.

dependencies.description: EN trends follow from electron configuration

compositionRules.yields: Complete periodic trend picture: size, EN, IE, EA all interrelated via effective nuclear charge

applicabilityPatterns: Predict the polarity of a chemical bond
Determine which atom attracts electrons more strongly in a molecule
Classify bond type from electronegativity difference

primitives.name: Ionization Energy
primitives.formalStatement: The first ionization energy IE_1 is the minimum energy required to remove the outermost electron from a neutral atom in the gas phase: X -> X^+ + e^-. Successive ionization energies IE_2, IE_3, ... are progressively larger. IE increases across a period and decreases down a group.
primitives.computationalForm: IE_1 measured in eV or kJ/mol. Hydrogen: 13.6 eV. Trends: across period, IE increases due to increasing effective nuclear charge. Down group, IE decreases due to increasing distance and shielding.

dependencies.description: IE depends on which orbital the electron is removed from

compositionRules.yields: Mulliken electronegativity: EN = (IE + EA)/2, connecting ionization and electron affinity
conditions: Atom with known IE and EA
compositionRules.example: Fluorine: IE = 17.4 eV, EA = 3.4 eV, Mulliken EN = (17.4 + 3.4)/2 = 10.4 eV

applicabilityPatterns: Determine how easily an atom loses an electron
Predict reactivity of metals (low IE = easily ionized)
Identify elements that form cations readily

primitives.name: Hund's Rule
primitives.formalStatement: For degenerate orbitals (same energy), electrons fill each orbital singly with parallel spins before any orbital gets a second electron. This maximizes total spin and minimizes electron-electron repulsion.
primitives.computationalForm: For a subshell with (2l+1) orbitals and k electrons (k <= 2l+1): place one electron in each orbital with same spin. For k > 2l+1: pair electrons in already-occupied orbitals. Example: 2p^3 -> one electron in each of three 2p orbitals, all spin-up.

dependencies.description: Hund's rule applies to degenerate orbitals within a subshell
Combined with Pauli exclusion to determine filling pattern

compositionRules.yields: Complete ground state configuration: Aufbau + Pauli + Hund's rule
compositionRules.example: Nitrogen (Z=7): 1s^2 2s^2 2p^3 -> three unpaired 2p electrons (not paired)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine the electron spin configuration in partially filled subshells
Predict whether an atom is paramagnetic (unpaired electrons)
Determine the ground state electron arrangement

primitives.name: Valence Electrons
primitives.formalStatement: Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost (highest n) shell of an atom. They determine the atom's chemical behavior: bonding, reactivity, and periodic group. For main group elements, the number of valence electrons equals the group number.
primitives.computationalForm: For main group: valence electrons = group number (Groups 1-2: s-electrons; Groups 13-18: s + p electrons). For transition metals: usually (n-1)d + ns electrons. Count from electron configuration.

dependencies.description: Valence electrons are in the outermost shell
Configuration determines which electrons are valence

compositionRules.yields: Lewis structures: represent bonding using valence electron dot diagrams
compositionRules.example: Carbon has 4 valence electrons, forms 4 bonds (CH_4, CO_2, etc.)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine how many bonds an atom typically forms
Predict the chemical behavior of an element from its group
Draw Lewis dot structures for molecules

primitives.name: Wave Function
primitives.formalStatement: The wave function psi(x,t) is a complex-valued function that completely describes the quantum state of a system. The probability of finding the particle between x and x+dx is |psi(x,t)|^2 dx. The wave function must be normalized: integral |psi|^2 dx = 1.
primitives.computationalForm: Probability density: rho(x) = |psi(x)|^2. Normalization: integral_{-inf}^{inf} |psi(x)|^2 dx = 1. Expectation value of observable A: <A> = integral psi* A_hat psi dx.

dependencies.description: Wave function evolves according to hbar in Schrodinger equation

compositionRules.yields: Quantum states as vectors in Hilbert space: psi is an element of L^2
compositionRules.example: Particle in a box: psi_n(x) = sqrt(2/L) sin(n*pi*x/L) are orthonormal basis vectors in L^2([0,L])

applicabilityPatterns: Describe the state of a quantum particle
Compute probabilities of measurement outcomes
Determine the time evolution of a quantum system
buildLabs: Build Lab 17.1: Wave Functions and Probability

primitives.name: Time-Independent Schrodinger Equation
primitives.formalStatement: For a stationary state with energy E: H_hat psi = E psi, where H_hat = -hbar^2/(2m) d^2/dx^2 + V(x) is the Hamiltonian operator. Solutions are energy eigenstates with definite energy E.
primitives.computationalForm: Solve the eigenvalue equation: -hbar^2/(2m) psi'' + V(x) psi = E psi. Boundary conditions determine allowed energies (quantization). For particle in a box: E_n = n^2*pi^2*hbar^2/(2mL^2).

dependencies.description: Equation governs the wave function
Uses the Hamiltonian operator
hbar appears in the kinetic energy operator

compositionRules.yields: Full quantum dynamics: stationary states evolve as psi(x,t) = psi(x) exp(-iEt/hbar)
compositionRules.example: Particle in a box: psi_n(x,t) = sqrt(2/L) sin(n*pi*x/L) exp(-iE_n*t/hbar)

applicabilityPatterns: Find the allowed energy levels of a quantum system
Solve for stationary states of a particle in a potential
Determine the energy spectrum of a quantum Hamiltonian
buildLabs: Build Lab 17.2: Solving the Schrodinger Equation

primitives.name: Time-Dependent Schrodinger Equation
primitives.formalStatement: The fundamental equation of quantum mechanics: i*hbar d/dt |psi(t)> = H_hat |psi(t)>. It governs the time evolution of any quantum state. The solution is |psi(t)> = exp(-iHt/hbar) |psi(0)>.
primitives.computationalForm: For time-independent H: expand psi(0) in energy eigenstates psi_n, then psi(t) = sum c_n psi_n exp(-iE_n*t/hbar). Time evolution operator: U(t) = exp(-iHt/hbar).

dependencies.description: Describes time evolution of the wave function
Uses the Hamiltonian operator

compositionRules.yields: Quantum dynamics: superposition of energy eigenstates oscillate at different frequencies
conditions: System not in an energy eigenstate
compositionRules.example: Superposition of two states: psi(t) = c_1 psi_1 e^{-iE_1 t/hbar} + c_2 psi_2 e^{-iE_2 t/hbar}

applicabilityPatterns: Determine how a quantum state changes over time
Compute transition probabilities between states
Describe quantum dynamics and oscillations

primitives.name: Hilbert Space
primitives.formalStatement: A Hilbert space H is a complete inner product space. In quantum mechanics, the state space is a complex Hilbert space where vectors represent quantum states and the inner product <psi|phi> gives probability amplitudes. L^2 (square-integrable functions) is the standard Hilbert space for position-space wave functions.
primitives.computationalForm: Inner product: <psi|phi> = integral psi*(x) phi(x) dx. Norm: ||psi|| = sqrt(<psi|psi>). Basis: {|n>} orthonormal if <m|n> = delta_{mn}. Completeness: sum |n><n| = I.

dependencies.description: Wave functions live in Hilbert space

compositionRules.yields: Operator algebra on Hilbert space: observables as self-adjoint operators with spectral decomposition
compositionRules.example: Position operator X psi(x) = x*psi(x) in L^2; momentum P psi(x) = -i*hbar d/dx psi(x)

applicabilityPatterns: Formalize the space in which quantum states live
Define inner products and orthogonality for quantum states
Establish the mathematical framework for quantum mechanics

primitives.name: Quantum Operators (Observables)
primitives.formalStatement: Observables in quantum mechanics are represented by Hermitian (self-adjoint) operators on Hilbert space. Position: X_hat psi = x*psi. Momentum: P_hat psi = -i*hbar*d/dx psi. The eigenvalues of an observable are the possible measurement outcomes.
primitives.computationalForm: Hermitian: A_hat = A_hat^dagger (self-adjoint). Eigenvalues are real. Eigenstates are orthogonal. Expectation value: <A> = <psi|A_hat|psi>. Commutator: [A,B] = AB - BA.

dependencies.description: Operators act on Hilbert space

compositionRules.yields: Complete measurement theory: operator eigenvalues are outcomes, |<eigenstate|psi>|^2 gives probability
conditions: Measuring observable A on state psi
compositionRules.example: Measure energy of particle in superposition: outcomes are E_n with probabilities |c_n|^2

applicabilityPatterns: Represent a physical measurement mathematically
Find the possible outcomes of a quantum measurement
Determine whether two observables can be measured simultaneously

primitives.name: Measurement Postulate (Born Rule)
primitives.formalStatement: When observable A with eigenstates {|a_n>} is measured on state |psi>, the outcome is one of the eigenvalues a_n with probability P(a_n) = |<a_n|psi>|^2. After measurement, the state collapses to |a_n>.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Expand |psi> in eigenbasis of A: |psi> = sum c_n |a_n>. 2. Probability of outcome a_n: P(a_n) = |c_n|^2. 3. Post-measurement state: |a_n>. Verify: sum P(a_n) = 1 (normalization).

compositionRules.yields: Probabilistic predictions: quantum mechanics predicts measurement statistics, not individual outcomes
compositionRules.example: Spin-1/2 in state |+x>: measure z-spin, get +hbar/2 or -hbar/2 each with probability 1/2

applicabilityPatterns: Predict the probability of each measurement outcome
Determine what happens to a quantum state after measurement
Calculate the statistics of repeated measurements

primitives.name: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
primitives.formalStatement: For any quantum state, the product of standard deviations of position and momentum satisfies: sigma_x * sigma_p >= hbar/2. More generally, for any two observables A, B: sigma_A * sigma_B >= |<[A,B]>|/2.
primitives.computationalForm: For position-momentum: Delta x * Delta p >= hbar/2. For energy-time: Delta E * Delta t >= hbar/2. Cannot simultaneously know both with arbitrary precision. Compute via sigma_A^2 = <A^2> - <A>^2.

dependencies.description: Uncertainty arises from non-commuting operators
General uncertainty principle involves the commutator
The bound involves hbar

compositionRules.yields: Fundamental limit on wave packet localization: narrower in x -> wider in p and vice versa
compositionRules.example: Gaussian wave packet: sigma_x * sigma_p = hbar/2 (minimum uncertainty state)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine the fundamental limits of simultaneous measurement precision
Estimate ground state energies from uncertainty arguments
Explain why electrons don't collapse into the nucleus

primitives.name: Superposition Principle
primitives.formalStatement: If |psi_1> and |psi_2> are valid quantum states, then any linear combination c_1|psi_1> + c_2|psi_2> (with |c_1|^2 + |c_2|^2 = 1 for normalization) is also a valid quantum state. This is a consequence of the linearity of the Schrodinger equation.
primitives.computationalForm: Combine states: |psi> = c_1|psi_1> + c_2|psi_2>. Normalize: ensure <psi|psi> = 1. Measurement probabilities: P(outcome associated with |psi_1>) = |c_1|^2.

compositionRules.yields: Interference: superposition creates non-classical probability patterns when measuring
compositionRules.example: Double slit: |psi> = (|left> + |right>)/sqrt(2), interference pattern P(x) = |psi_L(x) + psi_R(x)|^2

applicabilityPatterns: Combine quantum states to form new valid states
Describe a system that is in multiple states simultaneously
Explain quantum interference phenomena

primitives.name: Quantum State (Ket Notation)
primitives.formalStatement: A quantum state is represented by a ket |psi> in Hilbert space. The dual is a bra <psi|. The inner product <phi|psi> gives the probability amplitude for transitioning from |psi> to |phi>. The outer product |psi><phi| is an operator.
primitives.computationalForm: Ket: column vector |psi>. Bra: row vector <psi| = |psi>^dagger. Inner product: <phi|psi> = complex number. Outer product: |psi><phi| = matrix. Projection: P_psi = |psi><psi|.

dependencies.description: Kets are vectors in Hilbert space
Kets can be superposed as linear combinations

compositionRules.yields: Bra-ket formalism: compact notation for quantum mechanics: <psi|A|phi>
compositionRules.example: Expectation value of position: <psi|X|psi> = integral psi*(x) x psi(x) dx

applicabilityPatterns: Represent quantum states in Dirac notation
Compute inner products and probability amplitudes
Express quantum mechanical equations compactly

primitives.name: Expectation Value
primitives.formalStatement: The expectation value of observable A in state |psi> is <A> = <psi|A_hat|psi> = integral psi*(x) A_hat psi(x) dx. It represents the average outcome of many measurements of A on identically prepared systems.
primitives.computationalForm: In position basis: <A> = integral psi* A_hat psi dx. In eigenstate basis: <A> = sum |c_n|^2 a_n where c_n = <a_n|psi>. Standard deviation: sigma_A = sqrt(<A^2> - <A>^2).

dependencies.description: Expectation value is computed for operator A
Expectation value is the mean under Born rule probabilities

compositionRules.yields: Complete bra-ket expectation calculation: <psi|A|psi>
compositionRules.example: <x> for ground state of harmonic oscillator: <0|X|0> = 0 by symmetry

applicabilityPatterns: Compute the average result of measuring an observable
Determine the expected energy of a quantum system
Calculate the variance and uncertainty of a measurement

primitives.name: Commutator and Commutation Relations
primitives.formalStatement: The commutator of operators A and B is [A,B] = AB - BA. If [A,B] = 0, A and B commute and share eigenstates. The canonical commutation relation: [X,P] = i*hbar, encoding the fundamental quantum uncertainty.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute [A,B]psi = A(B psi) - B(A psi). Key relations: [X,P] = i*hbar, [L_x,L_y] = i*hbar*L_z, [a,a^dagger] = 1. If [A,B] != 0: cannot simultaneously diagonalize.

dependencies.description: Commutator is defined for quantum operators

compositionRules.yields: General uncertainty relation: sigma_A*sigma_B >= |<[A,B]>|/2
conditions: Operators A, B with nonzero commutator
compositionRules.example: [X,P] = i*hbar => sigma_x * sigma_p >= hbar/2

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if two observables can be simultaneously measured
Derive uncertainty relations from commutation relations
Establish the algebra of quantum mechanical operators

primitives.name: Quantum Harmonic Oscillator
primitives.formalStatement: The quantum harmonic oscillator with V(x) = (1/2)m*omega^2*x^2 has energy levels E_n = hbar*omega*(n + 1/2) for n = 0, 1, 2, ... The ground state energy E_0 = hbar*omega/2 is nonzero (zero-point energy). Solved via ladder operators a, a^dagger.
primitives.computationalForm: Ladder operators: a = sqrt(m*omega/(2*hbar))(x + ip/(m*omega)), a^dagger = conjugate. [a, a^dagger] = 1. H = hbar*omega*(a^dagger*a + 1/2). Raise: a^dagger|n> = sqrt(n+1)|n+1>. Lower: a|n> = sqrt(n)|n-1>.

dependencies.description: QHO is a solution of the time-independent Schrodinger equation
Ladder operator method uses [a, a^dagger] = 1

compositionRules.yields: Hermite-Gaussian wave functions: psi_n(x) = (m*omega/(pi*hbar))^{1/4} * H_n(xi) * exp(-xi^2/2) / sqrt(2^n * n!)
compositionRules.example: Ground state: Gaussian psi_0(x) = (m*omega/(pi*hbar))^{1/4} exp(-m*omega*x^2/(2*hbar))

applicabilityPatterns: Solve for energy levels of a particle in a quadratic potential
Model vibrations of atoms in molecules or crystals
Apply ladder operator techniques to raise/lower quantum states

primitives.name: Entanglement
primitives.formalStatement: Two quantum systems are entangled when the state of the composite system cannot be written as a tensor product of individual states: |psi_AB> != |psi_A> tensor |psi_B>. Measurement on one subsystem instantaneously determines the state of the other, regardless of distance.
primitives.computationalForm: Bell state example: |psi^+> = (|01> + |10>)/sqrt(2). Test: compute reduced density matrix rho_A = Tr_B(|psi><psi|). If rho_A is mixed (not pure), the state is entangled. Von Neumann entropy S = -Tr(rho_A log rho_A) > 0 for entangled states.

dependencies.description: Entangled states are superpositions of product states
Entanglement manifests through correlated measurement outcomes

compositionRules.yields: Bell states: maximally entangled 2-qubit states forming a basis
compositionRules.example: EPR pair: (|00> + |11>)/sqrt(2), measuring first qubit as 0 forces second to be 0

applicabilityPatterns: Describe correlations between quantum systems that have no classical explanation
Generate perfectly correlated measurement outcomes at a distance
Implement quantum teleportation or quantum key distribution
buildLabs: Build Lab 17.5: Entanglement and Bell States

primitives.name: Spin and Angular Momentum
primitives.formalStatement: Spin is an intrinsic angular momentum of quantum particles with no classical analog. For spin-s particles, the spin operators satisfy [S_x, S_y] = i*hbar*S_z (cyclic). Spin-1/2 particles (electrons): S_z has eigenvalues +hbar/2 and -hbar/2, represented by Pauli matrices.
primitives.computationalForm: Pauli matrices: sigma_x = [[0,1],[1,0]], sigma_y = [[0,-i],[i,0]], sigma_z = [[1,0],[0,-1]]. S = (hbar/2)*sigma. Spin-up: |+> = [1,0], spin-down: |-> = [0,1]. Total angular momentum: J = L + S.

dependencies.description: Spin algebra defined by commutation relations
Spin operators are quantum observables

compositionRules.yields: Spin-statistics theorem: half-integer spin -> fermion (Pauli exclusion), integer spin -> boson (no exclusion)
compositionRules.example: Electrons (spin-1/2): fermions, obey Pauli exclusion. Photons (spin-1): bosons, can occupy same state.

applicabilityPatterns: Describe the intrinsic angular momentum of particles
Compute spin states and measurement probabilities
Model magnetic properties of atoms and particles

primitives.name: Quantum Tunneling
primitives.formalStatement: A quantum particle can penetrate through a potential barrier V_0 > E, where E is the particle's energy. The tunneling probability through a rectangular barrier of width a is approximately T ≈ exp(-2*kappa*a) where kappa = sqrt(2m(V_0-E))/hbar.
primitives.computationalForm: For rectangular barrier of height V_0, width a, particle energy E < V_0: T ≈ 16*(E/V_0)*(1-E/V_0)*exp(-2*a*sqrt(2m(V_0-E)/hbar^2)). Exponential decay with barrier width and sqrt(mass * energy deficit).

dependencies.description: Tunneling is a solution of the Schrodinger equation with a barrier potential
Wave function has nonzero amplitude beyond the barrier

compositionRules.yields: Scale of tunneling: heavier particles or wider barriers suppress tunneling exponentially
conditions: Particle energy less than barrier height
compositionRules.example: Alpha decay: alpha particle tunnels through nuclear potential barrier, half-lives from microseconds to billions of years

applicabilityPatterns: Explain how particles pass through classically forbidden barriers
Calculate tunneling probabilities for quantum particles
Understand alpha decay and scanning tunneling microscopy
